overriding-and-specialization.label: Overriding and Specialization
overriding-and-specialization.summary: Keep one shared method name, then let each child class customize its own version of that method.

sketch0.title: One method name, shared by related classes

sketch1.title: Override the method in one subclass

try_overriding_and_specialization_sketch.title: Override label() for Book
try_overriding_and_specialization_sketch.prompt: The lesson used delivery items. Now practice with catalog media. In `models/book.py`, override `label()` so `Book("Project Hail Mary")` returns `Book: Project Hail Mary`. Then finish `main.py` by creating the book and printing its label.
try_overriding_and_specialization_sketch.hint: Use the same method name as the base class, but return a book-specific string.

help.concept: Overriding means a subclass writes its own version of a method with the same name. Callers still use `.label()`, but the subclass controls the result.

try_overriding_and_specialization_sketch.starterCode:
from models.book import Book

# TODO: create Book("Project Hail Mary").
# TODO: print the book label.

main_py.content:
from models.book import Book

# TODO: create Book("Project Hail Mary").
# TODO: print the book label.

models_book_py.content:
from models.catalog_item import CatalogItem


class Book(CatalogItem):
    # TODO: override label() so it returns "Book: <title>".
    pass

try_overriding_and_specialization_sketch.solutionCode:
from models.book import Book

book = Book("Project Hail Mary")
print(book.label())

main_py.content:
from models.book import Book

book = Book("Project Hail Mary")
print(book.label())

0.message: Book.label() should return the book-specific label.

try_overriding_and_specialization_sketch.title: Override label() for Movie
try_overriding_and_specialization_sketch.prompt: Use the same overriding pattern with another catalog type. In `models/movie.py`, override `label()` so `Movie("Inside Out")` returns `Movie: Inside Out`. Then finish `main.py` by creating the movie and printing its label.
try_overriding_and_specialization_sketch.hint: Book and Movie should both use `label()`, but each one can return a different kind of label.

help.concept: Two subclasses can share a method name while producing different results. That sets up polymorphism in the next topic.
help.hint_1: The method name should still be `label()`.

try_overriding_and_specialization_sketch.starterCode:
from models.movie import Movie

# TODO: create Movie("Inside Out").
# TODO: print the movie label.

main_py.content:
from models.movie import Movie

# TODO: create Movie("Inside Out").
# TODO: print the movie label.

models_movie_py.content:
from models.catalog_item import CatalogItem


class Movie(CatalogItem):
    # TODO: override label() so it returns "Movie: <title>".
    pass

try_overriding_and_specialization_sketch.solutionCode:
from models.movie import Movie

movie = Movie("Inside Out")
print(movie.label())

main_py.content:
from models.movie import Movie

movie = Movie("Inside Out")
print(movie.label())

0.message: Movie.label() should return the movie-specific label.

try_overriding_and_specialization_sketch.title: Print both specialized labels
try_overriding_and_specialization_sketch.prompt: Now use both specialized child classes. The `Book` and `Movie` classes are provided. In `main.py`, create `Book("Coraline")` and `Movie("Coco")`, then print each object's `label()` result on its own line.
try_overriding_and_specialization_sketch.hint: Create one Book and one Movie. Call `.label()` on each object.

help.concept: The call looks the same for both objects, but each subclass decides what its own label should look like.

try_overriding_and_specialization_sketch.starterCode:
from models.book import Book
from models.movie import Movie

# TODO: create a Book for "Coraline".
# TODO: create a Movie for "Coco".
# TODO: print each object's label on its own line.

main_py.content:
from models.book import Book
from models.movie import Movie

# TODO: create a Book for "Coraline".
# TODO: create a Movie for "Coco".
# TODO: print each object's label on its own line.

try_overriding_and_specialization_sketch.solutionCode:
from models.book import Book
from models.movie import Movie

book = Book("Coraline")
movie = Movie("Coco")
print(book.label())
print(movie.label())

main_py.content:
from models.book import Book
from models.movie import Movie

book = Book("Coraline")
movie = Movie("Coco")
print(book.label())
print(movie.label())

sc-overriding-purpose.title: Choose the reason to override
sc-overriding-purpose.hint: Look for the choice about changing the result while keeping the method name.

help.concept: Overriding keeps a shared contract but allows subclass-specific behavior.
help.hint_1: The method name stays the same.
help.hint_2: The returned text becomes more specific.

options.a: To remove inheritance from the program
options.b: To rename the base method everywhere
options.c: To keep `label()` but return a book-specific result
options.d: To stop `Book` objects from having titles

fb-overriding-method-name.title: Fill in the overriding method name
fb-overriding-method-name.prompt: Complete the method name that both subclasses share.
fb-overriding-method-name.hint: Use the same method name from the base class.

help.concept: Overriding works when the subclass uses the same method name as the base class.
help.hint_1: The method returns the display text for the item.
help.hint_2: It is not the constructor name.

dr-overriding-workflow.title: Order the override steps
dr-overriding-workflow.prompt: Put the beginner workflow in order.
dr-overriding-workflow.hint: Begin with the base method, then specialize it in subclasses.

help.concept: It is easiest to override after the base behavior already exists.
help.hint_2: Printing the result comes after the subclass method is ready.

tokens.t2: Create `Book.label()` with a book-specific return value
tokens.t3: Create `Movie.label()` with a movie-specific return value
tokens.t4: Make objects and print both labels

sc-overriding-shared-contract.title: Keep the shared contract
sc-overriding-shared-contract.prompt: What stays the same when `Book` and `Movie` both override `label()`?
sc-overriding-shared-contract.hint: Think about what later code can still call on both objects.

help.concept: The shared contract is the method name that every subclass supports.
help.hint_1: The result can change, but the callable behavior stays aligned.
help.hint_2: Look for the answer about one shared method name.

options.a: Later code can still call `label()` on both subclasses.
options.b: Both subclasses must return the exact same string.
options.c: Neither subclass can add its own behavior anymore.
options.d: The base class stops mattering once one subclass overrides a method.

sketch-0.title: Children can share the same method name

sketch-1.title: A child class can replace the parent method
sketch-1.bodyMarkdown:
Sometimes the parent method is too plain for a child class.

```python
class DeliveryTruck(DeliveryItem):
    def label(self):
        return f"Truck: {self.name}"
```

This is called **overriding**.

The child class writes a method with the same name as the parent method. Python uses the child version first.

The mental model:

- Parent gives the default behavior.
- Child can keep it.
- Child can also replace it when it needs a more specific result.

sketch-2.title: Different children can customize the same promise
